provenance.source_corpus: Hydrological changes following restoration of the Bois-des-Bel Peatland, Quebec, 1999–2002

source_spans.text: The restoration techniques contributed to the recovery of hydrological conditions necessary for Sphagnum recolonization
Restored site evaporation was 74%, 74%, and 98% of unrestored site evaporation in 2000, 2001, and 2002
The addition of the straw mulch contributed to a 13% and 8% decrease in evaporation relative to an adjacent unrestored section in 2000 and 2001
The construction of bunds have also contributed to increased retention of snowmelt water which prolongs soil wetness during the summer
The construction of bunds have also contributed to increased retention of snowmelt water which prolongs soil wetness during the summer
The construction of bunds have also contributed to increased retention of snowmelt water which prolongs soil wetness during the summer
Site runoff decreased in the years following restoration
during the summer period blocked ditches were generally effective at helping to reduce runoff losses from the restored site
Higher seasonal mean water table and soil moisture were observed at the restored site relative to the unrestored site in 2000 through 2002
Higher seasonal mean water table and soil moisture were observed at the restored site relative to the unrestored site in 2000 through 2002
Mean soil–water pressure was also higher than at the unrestored site
Seasonal soil volume change (swelling and shrinking of the peat) was evident at the restored site indicating rewetting of the peat profile
Seasonal soil volume change (swelling and shrinking of the peat) was evident at the restored site indicating rewetting of the peat profile
The restoration techniques contributed to the recovery of hydrological conditions necessary for Sphagnum recolonization
